chapter: Ch 7 — Fingerprints: Why Polynomials Catch Liars
stratum: Algebra I: fields & univariate polynomials (the engine's substrate)

phases.pre_rigorous: Picture a forger who hands you a giant filled-out spreadsheet and swears every cell obeys the rules. Re-checking the whole sheet is hopeless. So you do something almost insolent: you point at ONE random cell and check only that. The Schwartz–Zippel picture (lead with the figure: blue dots p, red dots q over F_101) shows why this works. Encode each side as a polynomial; two honest sides give the same polynomial, a forged side gives a different one. Two DIFFERENT low-degree polynomials are like two curves that can cross only a few times — over F_101 our p = 3x²+5x+7 and q = 3x²+2x+7 touch at exactly one point (x = 0) out of 101. So a finger dropped at random almost always lands where they disagree, and the lie is exposed. You did not need to read the whole sheet; you needed one lucky-by-design poke. (No symbols yet beyond naming p and q — the 'why' comes before the notation.)
phases.rigorous: Now earn it. Let F be a finite field and let p, q be polynomials over F of degree at most d with p ≠ q. Form the difference D = p - q. Because p ≠ q, D is a NONZERO polynomial of degree at most d. The factor / root-count fact says a nonzero degree-≤d polynomial has at most d roots in F. Each agreement point of p and q is exactly a root of D, so p and q agree at no more than d of the |F| field points. Draw r uniformly at random from F. Then Pr[p(r) = q(r)] = Pr[D(r) = 0] = (#roots of D)/|F| ≤ d/|F|. This destroys the bad intuitions explicitly: (1) 'passed one check ⇒ equal' is false — acceptance only bounds the error by d/|F|, never proves equality; (2) 'distinct degree-d polynomials can agree arbitrarily often' is false — the cap is exactly d, the degree of D; (3) the randomness lives in r, not in p or q, which are arbitrary fixed adversarial choices. For our pair, D = 3x has degree 1, so it has at most one root — realized at x = 0 — and the realized error is 1/101, comfortably under the degree-2 worst-case bound 2/101.
phases.post_rigorous: Rebuild the intuition on the rigor, both halves at once. The 'random cell catches a forged table' slogan IS the inequality Pr ≤ d/|F|, with the spreadsheet's algebraic identity playing the role of D. Pair each picture with its statement: the rare crossing point in the figure is a root of D; the 'one lucky poke' is the uniform draw of r; 'almost always disagree' is 1 - d/|F|. Now the soundness knob is obvious — to shrink the error you either grow |F| (the denominator rises while the ≤ d bad points stay put) or lower the encoding degree d. This is precisely the primitive beneath Layer 4: a whole-table claim (R1CS / AIR / PLONKish) collapses to one random evaluation, and sum-check and polynomial commitments are built on the same move. Keep one boundary sharp: this is an UNCONDITIONAL, root-counted collision bound, not a computational, assumption-based cryptographic hash — Ch 11/12 need the other kind, and conflating them corrupts those security models.
phases.you_could_have_invented_this: Knowing only 'a nonzero degree-d polynomial has ≤ d roots', you can invent the whole test: to check p = q, look at D = p - q; if D ≠ 0 it has ≤ d roots, so a random evaluation D(r) ≠ 0 catches the difference with probability ≥ 1 - d/|F|. That is Schwartz–Zippel — you derived it, you did not receive it.

heuristic_rigorous_pairs: Checking one random cell catches a forged spreadsheet.
For p ≠ q of degree ≤ d, Pr_{r∈F}[p(r)=q(r)] = Pr[D(r)=0] ≤ d/|F|, where D = p - q.
Two different low-degree curves can only cross a few times.
D = p - q is nonzero of degree ≤ d, so by the factor / root-count fact it has ≤ d roots; each root is an agreement point, hence ≤ d agreements.
A bigger field makes the test more reliable.
p and q are fixed; only |F| grows, so the bad-point fraction (#roots)/|F| ≤ d/|F| shrinks — the field size is the soundness knob.
Our two polynomials almost never agree (figure: one green point in 101).
D = 3x has degree 1, so exactly one root x = 0 over F_101; realized error 1/101 ≤ worst-case 2/101.

claims.claim: p(x) = 3x² + 5x + 7 and q(x) = 3x² + 2x + 7 are distinct degree-2 polynomials over F_101.
claims.evidence: Recipe constructs both; they differ only in the linear coefficient (5 vs 2).

claims.claim: p and q agree at exactly 1 point over F_101, namely x = 0.
claims.evidence: Sage: agreement_points = [0], num_agreements = 1. Independent Sage recompute over all of F_101 reproduces [0]. The manim scene recomputes the same set in pure Python and gets [0].

claims.claim: The difference p - q = 3x has degree 1 and its only root over F_101 is x = 0.
By Schwartz–Zippel, a uniformly random evaluation point fools the check (gives agreement) with probability at most d/|F| = 2/101 ≈ 1.98% for degree-2 inputs.
claims.evidence: d = 2, |F| = 101, d/|F| = 2/101 ≈ 0.0198. This is the WORST-CASE bound over all distinct degree-2 pairs. The realized agreement fraction for THIS pair is 1/101 ≈ 0.99%, smaller because p-q happens to have degree 1; both numbers are stated separately and neither is presented as the other.
claims.ledger_ref: Ch 7 'The lemma (C11): Schwartz-Zippel lemma (proved); vanishing-set fraction d/|F|'

claims.claim: Two distinct degree-d polynomials over F can agree on at most d points.
claims.source: factor / root-count fact applied to p - q
claims.evidence: p - q is a nonzero polynomial of degree ≤ d, hence has ≤ d roots; each root is an agreement point. Sage confirms the realized max here is 1 (= deg(p-q)).

adversarial_findings.finding: Potential conflation: the scene shows BOTH 'exactly 1 agreement point' and the bound 'd/|F| = 2/101'. A reader could think 1/101 should equal 2/101.
adversarial_findings.resolution: Kept both but labeled them as distinct quantities: 2/101 is the worst-case Schwartz–Zippel bound for any distinct degree-2 pair; 1/101 is the realized fraction for THIS pair, smaller because p-q = 3x has degree 1. Verified numerically in Sage (2/101 ≈ 0.0198 vs 1/101 ≈ 0.0099). Prose in draft.md states the distinction explicitly.

adversarial_findings.finding: Off-by-one / field-arithmetic risk in the agreement-point count.
adversarial_findings.resolution: Re-derived agreement set two independent ways (Sage loop over all of F_101; pure-Python loop in the manim scene); both yield [0], count 1. No off-by-one.

adversarial_findings.finding: Risk that the figure asset is empty or stale.
adversarial_findings.resolution: Re-ran the Sage recipe; assets/figures/schwartz_zippel.svg is 48,746 bytes and copied into figures/. No staleness.

comprehension_set.question: State the univariate Schwartz–Zippel bound: if p is a nonzero polynomial of degree d over a finite field F, what is the probability that p(r) = 0 for a uniformly random r in F, and why?
comprehension_set.answer_key: At most d/|F|. A nonzero degree-d polynomial has at most d roots (the factor / root-count fact), and r is drawn uniformly from |F| points, so the chance of hitting a root is (#roots)/|F| ≤ d/|F|.
comprehension_set.targets_stuck_point: ‘Two different degree-d polynomials can agree on arbitrarily many points’ — anchored by the ≤ d root count.
comprehension_set.on_miss_route_to: the root-count fact (factor theorem): a nonzero degree-d polynomial over a field has at most d roots

comprehension_set.question: Let p(x) = 3x² + 5x + 7 and q(x) = 3x² + 2x + 7 over F_101. At how many points do they agree, which point(s), and what is the chance a single random evaluation fails to distinguish them?
comprehension_set.answer_key: They agree where p - q = 3x = 0, i.e. only at x = 0, so exactly 1 agreement point. A single random r fails to distinguish them with probability 1/101 ≈ 0.99% (and never more than the degree-2 worst-case bound 2/101 ≈ 1.98%).
comprehension_set.targets_stuck_point: ‘Passed one check ⇒ equal’ — a single equality at x=0 does NOT mean p=q.
comprehension_set.on_miss_route_to: polynomials over a finite field (degree, roots, evaluation)

comprehension_set.question: A prover claims a giant computation's whole constraint table is satisfied. Why does checking one random field point catch a cheating prover with high probability, and what makes the guarantee shrink toward zero error?
comprehension_set.answer_key: The table identity is encoded as a polynomial identity P = 0; a cheating prover's P is a nonzero low-degree polynomial. By Schwartz–Zippel it is nonzero at all but ≤ d of the |F| points, so a random challenge exposes the cheat with probability ≥ 1 - d/|F|. Error shrinks by enlarging |F| (denominator grows while the ≤ d bad points stay fixed) or lowering the encoding degree d — the field size is the soundness knob.
comprehension_set.targets_stuck_point: ‘A bigger field makes the polynomials more different’ — it is the denominator |F| that grows, not the polynomials.
comprehension_set.on_miss_route_to: the ≤ d root-count fact (and, for many variables, the union bound)

comprehension_set.question: You could have invented this. Suppose you only know that a nonzero degree-d polynomial has at most d roots, and you want a cheap test that two polynomials p, q are equal. Derive a test and its failure probability from scratch — what do you evaluate, and how confident are you after one random check?
comprehension_set.answer_key: Form the difference D = p - q. If p = q then D ≡ 0; if p ≠ q then D is nonzero of degree ≤ d, so it has ≤ d roots. Pick r uniformly in F and test D(r) = 0. If D(r) ≠ 0 you are certain p ≠ q. If D(r) = 0 you accept p = q and are wrong only when r landed on one of the ≤ d roots — probability ≤ d/|F|. You have rediscovered Schwartz–Zippel: one random evaluation, error ≤ d/|F|, driven down by a bigger field.
comprehension_set.targets_stuck_point: ‘The lemma needs random/generic polynomials’ — the randomness is only in the evaluation point r; p and q are arbitrary fixed inputs.
comprehension_set.on_miss_route_to: modular arithmetic in F_p / finite fields
